{{Short description|Psychological theory}} 
The '''Marley hypothesis''' is a [[Psychology|psychological]] theory on how [[Race (human categorization)|racial]] groups may have different [[Perception|perceptions]] of the current [[racism]] in [[society]] due to a difference in their extent of knowledge and awareness of racial history. The study hypothesizes a strong correlation between how much one knows about past racial discrimination, and how well they recognize racism in a given situation.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Nelson|first1=Jessica C.|last2=Adams|first2=Glenn|last3=Salter|first3=Phia S.|s2cid=9727014|date=2012-12-11|title=The Marley Hypothesis|journal=Psychological Science|language=en|volume=24|issue=2|pages=213–218|doi=10.1177/0956797612451466|pmid=23232861|issn=0956-7976}}</ref> This study is executed through [[Social experiment|social experiments]], which theorizes how this [[Social behavior|behavioral pattern]] may arise due to members of a racial [[subgroup]] viewing [[Discrimination based on skin color|racial discrimination]] through a [[Sociocultural perspective|social lens]] that has been shaped by their past attuned knowledge and understanding of racist incidents in history. Researchers of the Marley Hypothesis propose that members of [[Minority group|minority groups]], the [[African Americans]], will have a higher understanding of racial history, while the [[Ethnic majority|majority]] group, European Americans, denies or overlooks such cases.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bonam|first1=Courtney M.|last2=Nair Das|first2=Vinoadharen|last3=Coleman|first3=Brett R.|last4=Salter|first4=Phia|s2cid=149419626|date=2018-02-16|title=Ignoring History, Denying Racism: Mounting Evidence for the Marley Hypothesis and Epistemologies of Ignorance|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|language=en|volume=10|issue=2|pages=257–265|doi=10.1177/1948550617751583|issn=1948-5506|url=https://cedar.wwu.edu/woodring_dei/3|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

== Original research ==
The original research for the Marley hypothesis was first proposed by scholars Jessica C. Nelson, Glenn Adams, and Phia Salter in 2012. They used a [[Detection theory|signal detection paradigm]] in their social experiments to explore their theory on how different groups perceive [[racism]] differently in a mirror reflection of their understanding of past racism.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Nelson|first1=Jessica C.|last2=Adams|first2=Glenn|last3=Salter|first3=Phia S.|s2cid=9727014|date=2012-12-11|title=The Marley Hypothesis|journal=Psychological Science|language=en|volume=24|issue=2|pages=213–218|doi=10.1177/0956797612451466|pmid=23232861|issn=0956-7976}}</ref> The experiment involved surveys completed by 199 [[European Americans|European American]] students from a predominantly White university and 74 [[African Americans|African American]] students from two [[Historically black colleges and universities|historically Black universities]]. The studies found that the European American participants did not notice racism as much as the African American participants when given incidents and systemic manifestations of racism to observe. In addition, a difference in the extent of how well they knew past incidents of racism was seen, where [[European Americans]] scored lower on historical knowledge of such cases.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Nelson|first1=Jessica C.|last2=Adams|first2=Glenn|last3=Salter|first3=Phia S.|s2cid=9727014|date=2012-12-11|title=The Marley Hypothesis|journal=Psychological Science|language=en|volume=24|issue=2|pages=213–218|doi=10.1177/0956797612451466|pmid=23232861|issn=0956-7976}}</ref> This correlates with the study's hypothesized idea that identity is relevant to perceptions of racism, where [[Institutional racism|systemic racism]] is present more strongly in self-image rather than isolated incidents.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Blodorn|first1=Alison|last2=O’Brien|first2=Laurie T.|last3=Kordys|first3=Justin|s2cid=145543255|date=2011-11-29|title=Responding to sex-based discrimination: Gender differences in perceived discrimination and implications for legal decision making|journal=Group Processes & Intergroup Relations|language=en|volume=15|issue=3|pages=409–424|doi=10.1177/1368430211427172|issn=1368-4302}}</ref> The study recognizes how a [[collective memory]] seen within a racial subgroup forms a [[collective identity]] which then embeds certain representations of history.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Liu|first1=James H.|last2=Hilton|first2=Denis J.|s2cid=16418164|date=2005-12-31|title=How the past weighs on the present: social representations of history and their role in identity politics|journal=The British Journal of Social Psychology|volume=44|issue=4|pages=537–556|doi=10.1348/014466605X27162|issn=0144-6665|pmid=16368018}}</ref>

[[File:Bob-Marley-in-Concert Zurich 05-30-80.jpg|thumb|Bob Marley in Zurich, 1980]]
The hypothesis was coined in respect to the singer [[Bob Marley]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bonam|first1=Courtney M.|last2=Nair Das|first2=Vinoadharen|last3=Coleman|first3=Brett R.|last4=Salter|first4=Phia|s2cid=149419626|date=2019-03-01|title=Ignoring History, Denying Racism: Mounting Evidence for the Marley Hypothesis and Epistemologies of Ignorance|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|language=en|volume=10|issue=2|pages=257–265|doi=10.1177/1948550617751583|issn=1948-5506|url=https://cedar.wwu.edu/woodring_dei/3|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Marley's lyrical and song compositions in the [[reggae]] genre were used to describe hope for those experiencing social oppression,<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://theconversation.com/under-the-influence-of-bob-marley-the-timeless-music-man-63347|title=Under the influence of ... Bob Marley, the timeless music man|last=Maganga|first=Stewart|date=2016-08-04|website=The Conversation|language=en|access-date=2019-05-15}}</ref> using his music as a medium for prophetic social criticism.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Black Heretics, Black Prophets|last=Bogues|first=Anthony|publisher=Routledge Publishing|year=2003|isbn=0415943256|location=New York|pages=190}}</ref> Marley is considered a popular singer and social icon rather than a social critic, as discourse around values and ideas are constantly changing and shifting.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Black Heretics, Black Prophets|last=Bogues|first=Anthony|publisher=Routledge Publishing|year=2003|isbn=0415943256|location=New York|pages=189}}</ref> His social influence grew after he used his music to spread the [[Rastafari]] religion in response to Jamaican activist [[Marcus Garvey]], who advocated on the ideologies of [[black nationalism]] and [[Pan-Africanism]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/religion/religions/rastafari/ataglance/glance.shtml|title=BBC - Religions - Rastafari: Rastafari at a glance|last=BBC|date=2009-10-02|website=www.bbc.co.uk|language=en-GB|access-date=2019-05-15}}</ref> 

Scholars perceive three major shifts at the [[wikt:macro-|macro]] level that influenced these racial perceptions within the United States. This includes the [[civil rights movement]] which emerged during the 1960s, the changes in the immigration policy in 1965 that led to an increased rate of [[Hispanic]] and [[Asian Americans|Asian]] immigrants, and the economical and political stability within less developed countries that led to [[mass migration]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Padilla|first=Amado M.|chapter=Social Cognition, Ethnic Identity, and Ethnic Specific Strategies for Coping with Threat due to Prejudice and Discrimination|date=2008|pages=7–42|editor-last=Willis-Esqueda|editor-first=Cynthia|series=THE NEBRASKA SYMPOSIUM ON MOTIVATION|publisher=Springer New York|language=en|doi=10.1007/978-0-387-73233-6_2|pmid=18232129|isbn=9780387732336|title=Motivational Aspects of Prejudice and Racism|volume=53}}</ref> As the ethnic identity of Americans gradually shifted during these three macro shifts, the social discourse surrounding racist behavior and prejudices took place. The following increase in diversity also increased negative responses to issues surrounding race and immigration among Americans.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Padilla|first=Amado M.|chapter=Social Cognition, Ethnic Identity, and Ethnic Specific Strategies for Coping with Threat due to Prejudice and Discrimination|date=2008|pages=7–42|editor-last=Willis-Esqueda|editor-first=Cynthia|series=THE NEBRASKA SYMPOSIUM ON MOTIVATION|publisher=Springer New York|language=en|doi=10.1007/978-0-387-73233-6_2|pmid=18232129|isbn=9780387732336|title=Motivational Aspects of Prejudice and Racism|volume=53}}</ref> 

=== Follow-up study ===
A follow-up study was conducted by scholars Courtney M. Bonam, Vinoadharen Nair Das, Brett R. Coleman, and Phia Salter, who underwent two major social experiments in a conceptual replication of Nelson, Adams, and Salter's work to further analyse the Marley hypothesis.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bonam|first1=Courtney M.|last2=Nair Das|first2=Vinoadharen|last3=Coleman|first3=Brett R.|last4=Salter|first4=Phia|s2cid=149419626|date=2018-02-16|title=Ignoring History, Denying Racism: Mounting Evidence for the Marley Hypothesis and Epistemologies of Ignorance|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|language=en|volume=10|issue=2|pages=257–265|doi=10.1177/1948550617751583|issn=1948-5506|url=https://cedar.wwu.edu/woodring_dei/3|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Study 1 analysed the responses of 111 European Americans and 43 African Americans, both from a racially diverse university. A similar pattern was seen with the original research were European Americans showed a lower understanding and knowledge of historical events involving racism.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bonam|first1=Courtney M.|last2=Nair Das|first2=Vinoadharen|last3=Coleman|first3=Brett R.|last4=Salter|first4=Phia|s2cid=149419626|date=2018-02-16|title=Ignoring History, Denying Racism: Mounting Evidence for the Marley Hypothesis and Epistemologies of Ignorance|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|language=en|volume=10|issue=2|pages=257–265|doi=10.1177/1948550617751583|issn=1948-5506|url=https://cedar.wwu.edu/woodring_dei/3|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Their work emphasizes how a strong racial identity among the European Americans may further implement their denial towards [[Institutional racism|systemic racism]]. Study 2 shows how people who had learned about the housing policy in U.S. history regarding its impact on African American [[Ghetto|ghettos]]<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.npr.org/2015/05/14/406699264/historian-says-dont-sanitize-how-our-government-created-the-ghettos|title=Historian Says Don't 'Sanitize' How Our Government Created Ghettos|last=Fresh Air|date=2015-05-14|newspaper=NPR.org|language=en|access-date=2019-05-08}}</ref> had a higher level of acknowledgement towards [[Institutional racism|systemic racism]].

The study revolves around the concepts of [[prejudice]] and [[discrimination]] shaping one's perspectives on the outlook of racist occurrences. These are built from social rules and [[Norms (sociology)|norms]] behind socially shared ideas within a specific group. Especially for the case of racism, prejudice is observed as being socio-historically constructed.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Barros|first1=Aluísio JD|last2=Faerstein|first2=Eduardo|last3=Gonçalves|first3=Helen|last4=Bastos|first4=João Luiz|date=2009-11-15|title=Experiences of discrimination among university students in the city of Rio de Janeiro|journal=Revista de Saúde Pública|language=en|volume=44|issue=1|pages=28–38|doi=10.1590/S0034-89102010000100003|pmid=20140326|issn=0034-8910|doi-access=free}}</ref> As a result, the social environment and historical racial events linking [[European Americans]] to racism is proposed to color how they perceive certain acts of racism. A lack of exposure to environments outside an individual from the [[wikt:privilege|privilege]]d group, especially regarding personal, micro-level interactions with the [[Minority group|minority]] group, can strengthen a certain bias or [[stereotype]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Barros|first1=Aluísio JD|last2=Faerstein|first2=Eduardo|last3=Gonçalves|first3=Helen|last4=Bastos|first4=João Luiz|date=2009-05-01|title=Experiences of discrimination among university students in the city of Rio de Janeiro|journal=Revista de Saúde Pública|language=en|volume=44|issue=1|pages=28–38|doi=10.1590/S0034-89102010000100003|pmid=20140326|issn=0034-8910|doi-access=free}}</ref> This perception strongly depends on the position a certain group is within the social structure. The prejudice towards the [[African Americans|African American]] community is likely to cause widespread social exclusion and devaluation, in comparison to the [[European Americans|European American]] community where the attributions to prejudice focuses more on localized reasons.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Schmitt|first1=Michael T.|last2=Branscombe|first2=Nyla R.|s2cid=143953546|date=2002-01-01|title=The Meaning and Consequences of Perceived Discrimination in Disadvantaged and Privileged Social Groups|journal=European Review of Social Psychology|volume=12|issue=1|pages=167–199|doi=10.1080/14792772143000058|issn=1046-3283}}</ref> The problem within this pattern is discussed to be how it impacts [[Self-perception theory|self-perception]] and assumed discrimination. These factors contribute to shaping perceptions on the current racist events placed in front of them. Social experiments found that the interpretation of discriminatory situations placed in front of people were influenced and shaped by subjective factors like personal interests and the level of interactions with other social subgroups.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Barros|first1=Aluísio JD|last2=Faerstein|first2=Eduardo|last3=Gonçalves|first3=Helen|last4=Bastos|first4=João Luiz|date=2009-11-15|title=Experiences of discrimination among university students in the city of Rio de Janeiro|journal=Revista de Saúde Pública|language=en|volume=44|issue=1|pages=28–38|doi=10.1590/S0034-89102010000100003|pmid=20140326|issn=0034-8910|doi-access=free}}</ref> [[Minority group|Disadvantaged]] groups are led to assume an inevitable [[Discrimination|discriminatory]] treatment towards them when placed within a wide range of social situations. However, the lower rate of discriminatory events privileged groups are faced with leads them to attribute their self-perceptions with an acknowledgement that treatment infrequently ends in a negative way. The differences in [[Self-perception theory|self-perceptions]] surrounding the consistency of discrimination emerging from [[Prejudice|prejudices]] means that groups at a lower social position may be more [[Psychology|psychologically]] affected. Scholars propose that the prejudice held towards members of a [[Minority group|minority]] group causes significantly higher damage to their psychological health, in comparison to more privileged groups.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Schmitt|first1=Michael T.|last2=Branscombe|first2=Nyla R.|s2cid=143953546|date=2002-01-01|title=The Meaning and Consequences of Perceived Discrimination in Disadvantaged and Privileged Social Groups|journal=European Review of Social Psychology|volume=12|issue=1|pages=167–199|doi=10.1080/14792772143000058|issn=1046-3283}}</ref>

== Sexism ==
The social pattern seen analysed in the Marley hypothesis is paralleled in other social groups, including gender. Scholars Diane Kobrynowicz and Nyla R. Branscombe discuss a similar case to the Marley Hypothesis with the idea that victims of [[Sexism|gender discrimination]] and social status views this idea differently.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kobrynowicz|first1=Diane|last2=Branscombe|first2=Nyla R.|s2cid=145365726|date=1997-09-01|title=Who Considers Themselves Victims of Discrimination?: Individual Difference Predictors of Perceived Gender Discrimination in Women and Men|journal=Psychology of Women Quarterly|language=en|volume=21|issue=3|pages=347–363|doi=10.1111/j.1471-6402.1997.tb00118.x|issn=0361-6843}}</ref> They expand on the thesis that perceptions of [[discrimination]] are shaped by the different structures of [[Social privilege|privileged]] and [[disadvantaged]] groups. This [[social experiment]] aims to predict how different levels of [[self-esteem]] and perceptions of [[sexism]] can shape how an individual responds to cases of gender discrimination, by observing a sample of 138 female and 157 male students.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kobrynowicz|first1=Diane|last2=Branscombe|first2=Nyla R.|s2cid=145365726|date=1997-09-01|title=Who Considers Themselves Victims of Discrimination? Individual Difference Predictors of Perceived Gender Discrimination in Women and Men|url=https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ554852|journal=Psychology of Women Quarterly|language=en|volume=21|issue=3|pages=347–363|issn=0361-6843|doi=10.1111/j.1471-6402.1997.tb00118.x|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Their pattern of behavior is analysed to understand a common observation that [[Social privilege|privileged]] groups, in comparison to [[disadvantaged]] groups, are given more positive outcomes because they are a member of a group holding higher positions of power in the root of the [[social structure]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Schmitt|first1=Michael T.|last2=Branscombe|first2=Nyla R.|last3=Kobrynowicz|first3=Diane|last4=Owen|first4=Susan|s2cid=145631935|date=2002-02-01|title=Perceiving Discrimination Against One's Gender Group has Different Implications for Well-Being in Women and Men|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|language=en|volume=28|issue=2|pages=197–210|doi=10.1177/0146167202282006|issn=0146-1672|url=https://kuscholarworks.ku.edu/bitstream/1808/258/1/Schmitt_Branscombe_perceiving.pdf|hdl=1808/258|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

The [[Sexism|gender discrimination]] within the workplace is often analysed through the disparities with wage and work position inequalities. Sociological scholars propose that cognitive, perceptual, and behavioral prejudices are prevalent within the workplace, impacting workplace factors like wages, promotions, or given roles and responsibilities. This is seen to emerge from influenced perceptions from workers due to the stereotyping of gender roles. Some scholars propose that [[African Americans]] display a higher sympathetic awareness of sexist occurrences because they are often exposed to a similar situation of prejudice and discrimination in the social environment.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Thomas|first=Charles B.|date=2001|journal=Michigan Sociological Review|volume=15|pages=10–28|issn=1934-7111|jstor=40969057|title=Characteristics Correlated with Perceptions of Gender Inequality}}</ref> This can influence how they perceive [[Sexism|gender discrimination]] in the workplace. Another subgroup who exhibits higher awareness surrounding sexist events is proposed to be generally younger people due to generational influences, where they tend to show a stronger sense of idealism and [[social justice]] for a more progressive society than the older generation.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Thomas|first=Charles B.|date=2001|journal=Michigan Sociological Review|volume=15|pages=10–28|issn=1934-7111|jstor=40969057|title=Characteristics Correlated with Perceptions of Gender Inequality}}</ref>

== Replication ==
In 2018, Jason E. Strickhouser, Ethan Zell, and Kara E. Harris published their attempt at replicating the Marley hypothesis and found a smaller difference between [[European Americans]]' and [[African Americans]]' perceptions of racism, as well as no difference between European American participants and African American participants when it came to knowledge on historical racism.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Strickhouser|first1=Jason E.|last2=Zell|first2=Ethan|last3=Harris|first3=Kara E.|s2cid=150030206|date=2018-10-22|title=Ignorance of History and Perceptions of Racism: Another Look at the Marley Hypothesis|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|volume=10|issue=7|language=en|pages=977–985|doi=10.1177/1948550618808863|issn=1948-5506}}</ref> The research team conducted two social experiments in a conceptual replication of the original study in different institutional and regional contexts with 228 European American students and 192 African American students. In one experiment, the effect of participants' race on their perception of racism was a third of the size compared to the prior studies' findings on isolated racism and around half for [[Institutional racism|systemic racism]], while another experiment found effect sizes that were a quarter of the original studies' regarding isolated racism and a third of the original studies' for systemic racism. Additionally, findings did not demonstrate that African American participants had significantly better knowledge of historical racism compared to European American participants. The research team concluded that while the Marley hypothesis did not replicate fully across all contexts, White Americans may have increased knowledge of historical racism and increased perceptions of racism when they have more frequent contact with Black Americans.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Strickhouser|first1=Jason E.|last2=Zell|first2=Ethan|last3=Harris|first3=Kara E.|s2cid=150030206|date=2018-10-22|title=Ignorance of History and Perceptions of Racism: Another Look at the Marley Hypothesis|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|volume=10|issue=7|language=en|pages=977–985|doi=10.1177/1948550618808863|issn=1948-5506}}</ref>

== See also ==
